title: User Management Setup
excerpt: User Management Setup GCXONE uses a Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) system to manage user permissions across the platform. Access is defined through roles, module privileges, and entity assignments — controlling what actions users can perform and wh
content:
User Management Setup GCXONE uses a Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) system to manage user permissions across the platform. Access is defined through roles, module privileges, and entity assignments — controlling what actions users can perform and which customers, sites, devices, or sensors they can access.
Step 1: Review Default Roles GCXONE provides predefined roles to simplify initial configuration:
Super Admin — full platform control across all modules and entities
Admin — administrative access for user management, configuration, and reporting
Operator — operational monitoring and alarm handling
Installer — device and sensor configuration access
End User — read-only access to dashboards, reports, and assigned devices These roles can be used directly or customized depending on operational requirements.
Step 2: Create or Modify Roles Navigate to Settings → Roles From this page you can:
Create new roles
Modify existing roles
Configure module privileges
Define entity access for customers, sites, and devices
Step 3: Invite Users Navigate to Settings → Users and invite users by email. During the invitation process:
A role is assigned to the user
Module privileges are inherited from the assigned role
Entity access is applied based on role configuration After invitation, users receive an email with instructions to activate their account and access the platform.
Next Step After configuring roles and inviting users, proceed with Device Integration & Testing to add and validate devices within the platform. For detailed RBAC configuration and role management, refer to User Management.

title: What is GCXOne
excerpt: What is GCXONE? GCXONE is an AI-powered, cloud-native security operations platform that unifies video surveillance, alarm management, and IoT monitoring into a single intelligent interface — without requiring any local infrastructure. It is designed
content:
What is GCXONE? GCXONE is an AI-powered, cloud-native security operations platform that unifies video surveillance, alarm management, and IoT monitoring into a single intelligent interface — without requiring any local infrastructure. It is designed for security operations centers, service providers, and enterprises that need to monitor multiple sites, manage alarms efficiently, and scale their operations without complexity.
The Problem GCXONE Solves Traditional security operations struggle with:
Alarm fatigue — high volumes of false alarms that overwhelm operators
Slow investigations — manual workflows that delay response
Blind spots — cameras going offline unnoticed
Complex onboarding — adding new customers and sites takes too long
Limited visibility — no unified view across distributed locations GCXONE was built specifically to solve these challenges.
How GCXONE Works
Devices send events — cameras, sensors, VMS systems, and alarm panels connect to GCXONE
AI filters and classifies — irrelevant events are removed, real threats are prioritized
Operators take action — verified alarms are presented with video context and clear workflows
Alarms are resolved — through assignment, escalation, reporting, and audit trail
What GCXONE Delivers
Reduce Alarm Fatigue AI-driven filtering reduces false alarms by up to 99%, so operators focus only on genuine threats — not noise.
NOVA99x — filters out wind, shadows, and animals
Behavioral Analysis — detects anomalies in system health and user access patterns
Proactive Mitigation — identifies risks before they escalate into incidents
Improve Operational Efficiency
ZenMode — compiled alarm summaries for faster review
Universal Search — find anything instantly
Automated Triage — automation resolves routine service requests, reducing manual overhead by up to 70%
Role-based dashboards — each user sees only what is relevant to them
Increase Uptime Health Check proactively monitors device connectivity, black screen detection, obstruction, and low-light conditions — catching issues before they impact operations.
Enable Scalable Growth
Bulk Import — onboard customers and sites at scale
Tower Guard — centralized mobile tower monitoring
Marketplace — activate features instantly without deployments
Platform Architecture GCXONE runs on a secure multi-tenant SaaS architecture, meaning each organization's data is fully isolated, secure, and accessible only to authorized users. The platform hierarchy:
Tenant — the service provider or top-level organization
Customer — end-client organizations managed by the tenant
Site — physical locations belonging to a customer
Device — cameras, sensors, and panels at each site
Sensor — individual detection points on a device
Multi-Level Operational Control
Service Provider Level — full visibility across all customers
Customer Level — oversight of all sites and devices
Site Level — real-time monitoring of a specific location
Device Level — individual device health and event history
Role-Aware by Design Every user sees only the modules, dashboards, and entities relevant to their role. Access is controlled through structured role configuration and entity-level permissions — ensuring clarity, security, and efficiency.
RBAC — granular Role-Based Access Control
Audit Trails — comprehensive logging of all system activity for compliance needs
AI at the Core GCXONE includes Genie, an embedded AI assistant that:
Answers operational questions in plain language
Provides analytics and performance insights
Assists with troubleshooting
Integrates with ticketing systems and workflows
Integration with TALOS GCXONE works alongside Evalink TALOS as part of a complete operational workflow. GCXONE handles AI filtering and alarm prioritization — only validated alarms are forwarded to TALOS for structured operator handling and escalation.
Technology Foundation
Deployment — 100% cloud-based, zero on-premise hardware
Uptime — 99.9% SLA on AWS infrastructure
Encryption — end-to-end, data encrypted at rest and in transit
Compliance — ISO 27001 certified
Integration — REST APIs and webhooks for third-party systems
Device Support — 200+ manufacturers including Hikvision, Axis, Dahua
ROI & Business Value
Total Cost of Ownership — 40% reduction by eliminating server and maintenance costs
Administrative Time — 70% saved through automation and unified UI
Incident Response — 50% faster with real-time AI alerts and triage
CapEx Investment — zero, fully cloud-based deployment

title: Bandwidth Requirements
excerpt:
Bandwidth Requirements Reference Proper bandwidth planning is essential for reliable video surveillance. This reference provides the formulas and metrics needed to calculate capacity for your GCXONE deployment.
Calculation Formula Use this formula to
content:
Bandwidth Requirements Reference Proper bandwidth planning is essential for reliable video surveillance. This reference provides the formulas and metrics needed to calculate capacity for your GCXONE deployment.
Calculation Formula Use this formula to estimate the total bandwidth required for your installation: Total Bandwidth = (Number of Cameras × Avg Bitrate) + (Concurrent Viewers × Streaming Bitrate) + 30% Management Overhead
Bitrate Guidelines Bandwidth consumption varies significantly based on resolution and the codec used. H.265 (HEVC) is highly recommended for 50% bandwidth savings. Installation Examples
🏠 Small (10 Cameras)
Resolution: 1080p (H.265)
Upload Required: ~30 Mbps
Viewers: 2 Concurrent Streams
Total Recommended: 50 Mbps Symmetric
🏢 Medium (50 Cameras)
Resolution: Mixed 1080p/4MP (H.265)
Upload Required: ~200 Mbps
Viewers: 10 Concurrent Streams
Total Recommended: 400 Mbps Symmetric
🏭 Large (200+ Cameras)
Resolution: Mixed (H.265)
Upload Required: ~1 Gbps
Total Recommended: 2 Gbps Dedicated Link
Optimization Strategies
Use H.265 Everywhere: Ensure your cameras and NVRs are set to HEVC/H.265 to cut data costs.
Motion-Based Bitrate: Configure Variable Bitrate (VBR) so the stream only spikes during actual activity.
Sub-Streaming: Use a lower resolution "Sub-Stream" for multi-camera grid views, and only switch to "Main Stream" for full-screen inspection.
VLAN Segmentation: Keep video traffic on a dedicated VLAN to prevent congestion with general office data.
Common Issues
Freezing/Artifacts: Usually caused by sustained upload congestion. Try reducing the frame rate from 30 fps to 15 fps.
Delayed Alarms: Often a result of network saturation. Implement Quality of Service (QoS) to prioritize alarm handshake traffic (Port 443).

content:

Without whitelisting the primary gateway IPs, the GCXONE platform will be unable to receive alarms or heartbeat signals from your hardware.
🌐 Mandatory IP Addresses These addresses must be whitelisted for Outbound traffic from your device network.
Primary Gateways Service IP Address Purpose GCXONE Gateway (Primary) Main platform communication GCXONE Gateway (Backup) Failover and redundancy Messaging Services Secure event delivery
Video Streaming Servers Service IP Address Usage Streaming Node 1 Primary WebRTC node Streaming Node 2 Fallback streaming Streaming Node 3 Regional overflow
🔌 Required Ports & Protocols Ensure these ports are open for the specified protocols. Port Range Protocol Service Usage 80 HTTP Web Redirects Initial connection handshake 443 HTTPS/WSS Core API Secure dashboard & WebSockets 554 RTSP Video Stream Fallback for legacy viewers 5671 / 5672 AMQPS Messaging Secure event delivery (TLS) 10001 - 10500 UDP/TCP WebRTC Dynamic ports for video streaming 123 UDP NTP Time synchronization WebRTC Port Range: The 10001-10500 range is required for peer-to-peer video negotiation, ensuring the lowest possible latency during live monitoring.

title: Browser Errors
excerpt:
🍪 Resolving Browser Cookie Errors If you can access the Genesis Dashboard but receive an "Oops something went wrong" message when clicking on Alarm Management or Workflows, the issue is almost certainly your browser's security settings.
🔍 The Root
content:
🍪 Resolving Browser Cookie Errors If you can access the Genesis Dashboard but receive an "Oops something went wrong" message when clicking on Alarm Management or Workflows, the issue is almost certainly your browser's security settings.
🔍 The Root Cause Talos is an embedded application. It runs inside the Genesis interface but communicates with a separate security backend. To maintain your session across these two domains, your browser must allow third-party cookies.
🛠️ How to Fix: Quick Toggle The fastest way to fix this is using the address bar toggles:
Look at the right side of your Browser Address Bar.
Click the Eye icon (👁️) or Cookie icon.
Toggle the switch to "Allow Third-Party Cookies" for this site.
Refresh the page.
⚙️ Manual Browser Settings If the quick toggle isn't visible, use the manual settings for your specific browser:
Go to → → . 2. Select Allow third-party cookies. 3. Alternatively: Add and to the "Allowed to use third-party cookies" list. 1. Go to → . 2. Ensure "Allow sites to save and read cookie data" is On. 3. Ensure "Block third-party cookies" is Off. 1. Go to (or Preferences) → . 2. Uncheck "Prevent cross-site tracking". 3. Uncheck "Block all cookies". 💡 Troubleshooting Checklist
Incognito Mode: If Talos works in Incognito, clear your regular browser cache.
Ad-Blockers: Some ad-blockers (like uBlock Origin) treat Talos cookies as "trackers." Whitelist the site in your extension settings.
Corporate VPN: Some VPNs strip cookie headers. Try connecting from a direct internet line to rule out network-level blocking.

title: Glossary of Terms
excerpt:
Glossary of Terms This glossary provides definitions for common terms used within the GCXONE platform and the wider security industry.
Platform Hierarchy & Core Concepts
Tenant: The top-level organization (e.g., a Monitoring Station or Service Provid
content:
Glossary of Terms This glossary provides definitions for common terms used within the GCXONE platform and the wider security industry.
Platform Hierarchy & Core Concepts
Tenant: The top-level organization (e.g., a Monitoring Station or Service Provider).
Customer: The end-user or client organization under a Tenant.
Site: A physical location being monitored (e.g., "Main Warehouse").
Device: The main hardware unit (gateway, NVR, DVR) connected to GCXONE.
Sensor / Camera: Individual channels or sensors attached to a Device.
Workflow: A predefined sequence of automated actions triggered by an alarm.
Operational Terms
ARM / DISARM: Enabling or disabling alarm reporting for a site or device.
Isolate: Temporarily bypassing a specific sensor or camera from triggering alarms.
Site Pulse / Lifecheck: A regular "heartbeat" signal sent by a device to verify connectivity.
Technical Terminology
NVR (Network Video Recorder): A specialized computer system that records video in a digital format to a disk drive.
RTSP (Real-Time Streaming Protocol): A network control protocol designed for use in entertainment and communications systems to control streaming media servers.
SIP (Session Initiation Protocol): A signaling protocol used for initiating, maintaining, and terminating real-time sessions that include voice, video, and messaging applications.
Webhook: A method of augmenting or altering the behavior of a web page or web application with custom callbacks.
PTZ (Pan-Tilt-Zoom): Controls for moving a camera and adjusting its focal length.
I/O (Input/Output): Ports used for connecting external sensors (Inputs) and triggering relays (Outputs).
Analytics: Automated video analysis such as motion detection, human/vehicle classification, and line crossing.
Alarm Filtering: Logic and algorithms used to reduce false alarms by verifying events.
Need Help? If you're experiencing issues, check our Troubleshooting Guide or contact support.

title: Image & Video Display Issues
excerpt:
🖼️ Image & Video Display Issues This guide provides a systematic approach to resolving common visual artifacts, missing alarm snapshots, and streaming failures in the GCXONE platform.
❌ Missing Alarm Snapshots If alarms are triggered but the Video A
content:
🖼️ Image & Video Display Issues This guide provides a systematic approach to resolving common visual artifacts, missing alarm snapshots, and streaming failures in the GCXONE platform.
❌ Missing Alarm Snapshots If alarms are triggered but the Video Activity buffer shows empty image slots, verify the following:
Check Time Synchronization Discrepancies between the device clock and GCXONE (even 5-10 seconds) are the primary cause of image retrieval failure.
Ensure the device is using or .
DST Check: Ensure Daylight Saving Time is toggled ON. Without this, the system may fail during biannual time shifts.
Verify Recording Duration Short recording buffers can cause retrieval misses.
Recommended Setting: Configure your device to record at least 30 seconds of video per event (not 10 seconds). This provides enough "padding" to capture three reliable snapshots even if clocks are slightly out of sync.
Storage Health (HDD Failure) Check the device dashboard for HDD Failure or Disk Full alerts.
If the hard drive is failing, the device will stop buffering video, and all subsequent alarms will have "Empty" image evidence.
🔁 Incorrect/Mixed Alarm Images In rare cases, an alarm from "Site A" may display an image from "Site B." This is usually a symptom of Central Cache Corruption.
Scope Check: Determine if the issue affects a single camera or the entire site.
Root Cause: If images are routinely swapped across different physical NVRs, the local metadata index or the cloud cache index is likely out of sync.
Resolution: Document the affected sites and escalate to the Technical Systems Team for a cache-flush operation.
📹 Streaming Failures (Local Mode) If "Cloud Mode" works but "Local Mode" fails to load live video:
H.265 vs H.264 GCXONE cloud mode handles H.265 transcoding server-side. However, Local Mode sends raw packets directly from the Edge device to your browser.
Check Encoding: Verify if the camera is set to H.265.
Install Codec: Ensure your Windows operating system has the H.265 video extensions installed.
Switch to H.264: If the client machine cannot be updated, switch the camera's sub-stream encoding to H.264 for maximum compatibility.
IP Camera Limitations Independent IP cameras have limited processing power and often restrict the number of concurrent RTSP connections.
If multiple operators are viewing the same IP camera, the device may refuse further connections.
Solution: Switch the device to Cloud Only Mode. This routes all requests through a single cloud bridge, bypassing the device's connection limit.
💡 Quick Diagnostics Table Symptom Probable Cause Immediate Action Blue/Black Screen H.265 Codec Missing Install extensions or switch to H.264. "No Image" Button Time Sync Drifting Re-sync device via NTP. Site A shows Site B Cache Corruption Escalate to Tech Team. Laggy/Stuttering Low Upload Bandwidth Reduce sub-stream bitrate/FPS.

title: Platform Architecture Overview
excerpt:
NXGEN is built on a modern, scalable architecture designed for reliability and performance.
Core Components The platform consists of several key components that work together:
content:
NXGEN is built on a modern, scalable architecture designed for reliability and performance.
Core Components The platform consists of several key components that work together:

title: Alarm Flow
excerpt:
Alarm Flow Once an event is classified as a Real Alarm, it enters the alarm flow — the structured process that ensures every genuine security threat is received, assigned, and resolved by the right operator at the right time.
Alarm Trigger An alarm i
content:
Alarm Flow Once an event is classified as a Real Alarm, it enters the alarm flow — the structured process that ensures every genuine security threat is received, assigned, and resolved by the right operator at the right time.
Alarm Trigger An alarm is triggered when one of the following occurs: A camera or sensor detects a real security event that passes Nova 99x AI filtering. A Health Check failure is detected, such as a camera going offline, obstruction, black screen, or low light condition. A manual alarm is triggered by an operator or system administrator.
Alarm Delivery Confirmed alarms are delivered to the operator queue in Talos in real time. Each alarm arrives with full context including the customer and site name, device and sensor reference, event timestamp, alarm classification, and a GIF preview or video clip for fast review.
Operator Queue Inside Talos, operators see a live queue of incoming alarms. Each alarm in the queue can be assigned to a specific operator, preventing duplicate handling. The queue displays who is currently working on each alarm, making team coordination visible at a glance.
Alarm Review The operator opens the alarm and reviews the available information: GIF Preview: A short animated preview of the event for instant context. Jump Back: Replay the 30 seconds before the alarm was triggered. Live View: Switch to a live stream of the camera in real time. Playback: Review recorded footage around the event. Area Masking: Apply a temporary or permanent mask to a specific zone if needed.
Alarm Actions After reviewing the alarm, the operator takes one of the following actions: Confirm as Real: Escalate the alarm and trigger the configured workflow — such as calling a technician, sending an SMS or email, or contacting the customer. Mark as False: Close the alarm as a false positive. This data feeds back into Nova 99x analytics. Escalate: Assign the alarm to a supervisor or higher-level operator.
Workflow Automation GCXONE integrates with Talos workflows to automate alarm response. Depending on the alarm type and site configuration, workflows can automatically notify the customer, dispatch a technician, send an SMS or email alert, escalate to a supervisor, or log the response for SLA reporting.
Alarm Resolution & Audit Every alarm is closed with a resolution note and stored in the Audit Log with full details including the operator who handled it, the time of action, the steps taken, and the final resolution. This data is available for reporting, SLA validation, and compliance audits.

title: Cloud Architecture
excerpt:
Cloud Architecture GCXONE is built on modern cloud-native principles to provide a unified Video Surveillance as a Service (VSaaS) and IoT security management ecosystem.
The Core VSaaS Model GCXONE operates entirely in the cloud, eliminating the compl
content:
Cloud Architecture GCXONE is built on modern cloud-native principles to provide a unified Video Surveillance as a Service (VSaaS) and IoT security management ecosystem.
The Core VSaaS Model GCXONE operates entirely in the cloud, eliminating the complexity of traditional on-premise systems. By shifting to a SaaS model, organizations gain agility, scalability, and freedom from hardware maintenance. No Local Servers: Eliminates dedicated local NVR/DVR maintenance. Infinite Scalability: Add sites and cameras instantly without hitting physical hardware limits. Centralized Management: Deploy security patches and features globally in seconds.
Infrastructure Stack (AWS) The platform is hosted on Amazon Web Services (AWS) using a containerized microservices architecture managed by Kubernetes (EKS). Compute & Orchestration Private EKS Clusters: Core application logic runs on secure, private worker nodes. Auto-Scaling: Node groups adjust resources in real-time based on traffic and processing load. Messaging: Uses Amazon MQ for asynchronous event synchronization across the global platform. Storage & Data Asset Storage: Snapshots and icons are stored in encrypted S3 Buckets. Metadata Management: Uses scalable NoSQL databases for lightning-fast camera metadata retrieval.
The Universal Translator (Proxy Layer) The Proxy Architecture is GCXONE's core innovation. It allows the platform to communicate with diverse hardware manufacturers — Hikvision, Dahua, Axis, and others — by translating their proprietary protocols into a standardized unified command set. The flow works as follows: Diverse hardware communicates via SDK or ONVIF to the Proxy Layer, which acts as a translation engine. The Proxy Layer converts this into a standardized stream using WebRTC or RTSP, which then feeds into the GCXONE Core for AI processing and display. The Proxy Layer removes the need for expensive hardware bridges. Your cameras talk directly to the cloud via the GCXONE gateway.
Multi-Tenant Logical Hierarchy Data isolation is enforced through a strict multi-tenant model. Each organization lives in a dedicated Tenant space, ensuring data privacy and granular access control. The hierarchy is structured as follows: Tenant: The monitoring station or master organization. Customer: Individual client organizations under the tenant. Site: Physical facilities such as offices or warehouses. Device: NVRs or Gateways located at the site. Sensor: Individual camera channels or PIR sensors connected to a device.
Security & Compliance Security is integrated at the architectural level, not added as a bolt-on feature. Encryption: Data at rest and in transit is secured with AES-256. Access Gating: Jump Servers and private VPCs isolate infrastructure from the public web. Authentication: Integrated with Auth0 for enterprise-grade MFA and SSO. Continuous Audit: Every system call and operator action is logged in an immutable audit trail.

title: Event Processing
excerpt: Event Processing Understanding how GCXONE processes events is essential for operators and administrators. Every alarm you see on the dashboard has passed through a structured processing pipeline — from the moment a camera detects movement to the poin
content:
Event Processing Understanding how GCXONE processes events is essential for operators and administrators. Every alarm you see on the dashboard has passed through a structured processing pipeline — from the moment a camera detects movement to the point an operator takes action.
Event Lifecycle Every event in GCXONE follows a defined lifecycle: Detection: A camera or sensor detects an activity and sends a signal to the GCXONE platform via the Proxy Layer. Ingestion: The platform receives the raw event and logs it with a timestamp, device ID, site, and customer reference. AI Analysis: The event is passed through the Nova 99x AI engine, which classifies it as a Real Alarm, False Alarm, or Technical Event. Routing: Confirmed real alarms are routed to the operator queue via Talos for action. Resolution: The operator reviews, processes, and closes the event. All actions are logged in the Audit Trail.
Event Types GCXONE classifies every incoming event into one of three categories: Real Alarm: A genuine security event that requires operator attention, such as motion detection, line crossing, or intrusion. False Alarm: An event triggered by non-threatening activity such as animals, weather, or lighting changes. Filtered out by Nova 99x to reduce operator workload. Technical Event: A system-generated event triggered by Health Check, such as a camera going offline, a black screen, obstruction detection, or low light conditions.
AI Processing — Nova 99x Before any event reaches an operator, it passes through Nova 99x — GCXONE's AI filtering engine. Nova 99x analyzes each event using behavioral analysis and image recognition to determine whether it represents a real threat. This process reduces false alarm volume by up to 99%, freeing operators to focus only on events that matter. Filtering can be applied across thermal and optical image types.
Event Routing Once classified, events are routed based on their type and priority: Real Alarms are pushed to the operator queue in Talos, where they can be assigned, reviewed, and actioned according to configured workflows. False Alarms are filtered out and stored for reporting and analytics. They do not reach the operator queue. Technical Events trigger automated notifications and can initiate workflows such as calling a technician, sending an SMS or email, or escalating to a supervisor.
Event States Every event moves through the following states during its lifecycle: New: Event received and pending review. Filtered: Classified as a false alarm by Nova 99x and removed from the operator queue. Assigned: Allocated to a specific operator for action. In Progress: Operator is actively reviewing the event. Processed: Event has been reviewed and closed.
Event Data & Audit Every event is stored with full metadata including timestamp, device and sensor reference, site and customer, event type and classification, operator actions, and resolution notes. This data is available for reporting, SLA validation, and compliance audits through the Audit Log.

title: GCXONE & Talos Interaction
excerpt: GCXONE & Talos Interaction GCXONE acts as the Intelligent Processing Layer for Talos. While Talos handles core alarm distribution and operator workflows, GCXONE provides the computer vision and AI needed to verify threats, manage video streams, and s
content: GCXONE & Talos Interaction GCXONE acts as the Intelligent Processing Layer for Talos. While Talos handles core alarm distribution and operator workflows, GCXONE provides the computer vision and AI needed to verify threats, manage video streams, and synchronize site metadata across the ecosystem. Automatic Site Synchronization When an administrator creates a site in GCXONE, the platform automatically generates a corresponding record in Talos via MQTT messaging. Bidirectional Sync: Updates to site names, addresses, or contact details in GCXONE are pushed to Talos in real time. Test Mode Intelligence: If a site in Talos is set to Test Mode during maintenance, GCXONE automatically disarms that site to prevent unnecessary AI processing. The Alarm Lifecycle The most critical interaction between GCXONE and Talos occurs during a security event. The flow works as follows: Detection: A hardware device detects a breach and sends a signal to Talos. Forwarding: Talos uses a pre-configured workflow to forward images or video clips to the GCXONE AI engine. Verification: GCXONE's AI classifies the event based on configured priority, white, and black lists. Non-threatening movements such as shadows or animals are filtered out. Response: The operator receives a verified alarm in Talos, complete with bounding boxes showing exactly where the threat was detected. GCXONE's AI reduces false alarms by approximately 80%, ensuring operators focus only on genuine security threats. Key Automation Workflows Arm/Disarm Schedules: GCXONE can automate the arming and disarming of sites based on client business hours. When the schedule reaches the arming time, GCXONE calls the device API and waits 30 seconds for a status confirmation. If the device fails to arm, an Arming Failure alert is sent to the operator. Event Overflow Protection: To prevent system overload from faulty sensors, GCXONE implements overflow protection. If 25 or more alarms are received from a single sensor within 5 minutes, alarms are temporarily discarded and a maintenance ticket is generated automatically.

title: Getting to Know Talos
excerpt: Getting to Know Talos Evalink Talos is the primary interface where operators handle alarms and managers organize their customer base. This guide walks you through the core navigation and management concepts. Architecture & Reliability Talos is built
content: Getting to Know Talos Evalink Talos is the primary interface where operators handle alarms and managers organize their customer base. This guide walks you through the core navigation and management concepts. Architecture & Reliability Talos is built on a cloud-native microservices architecture. Every feature — from alarm processing to site management — runs as an independent service on Amazon Web Services (AWS). Global Redundancy: Servers are distributed across America, Europe, and Asia. Fail-Safe Processing: If a specific microservice encounters an issue, the rest of the platform continues to operate without interruption. Silent Updates: Security patches and feature releases are deployed remotely. When a major change occurs, you will see a prompt to reload the page to apply the update immediately. Navigating the Dashboard When you log in, you are greeted with the Main Dashboard providing a high-level overview of your security health. Alarm Queue: Real-time view of incoming alerts. My Assigned Alarms: Tracks incidents currently under your review. What's New: Click your Profile Icon then What's New to see the latest feature releases and security patches. Understanding Sites in Talos In Talos, a Site is any entity that can generate an alarm — a building, a specific floor, or even a personal panic button. Each site has one of three statuses: Active: Fully operational. Alarms are processed and recorded. Inactive: No alarms are accepted. Inactive sites do not count toward billing. Test Mode: Used during maintenance. Motion alerts can be ignored while keeping fire alarms active. Organizing Your Workspace As your organization grows, use Talos grouping features to keep data structured: Organizational Units: The highest level of grouping, such as a regional division. Site Groups: Regional or functional clusters, such as a group of retail locations in a specific area. Service Companies: Link external technicians or guard services to specific sites for automated task routing. Creating a Site in Talos Go to the Sites page and click Create Site. Choose an ID method — enter a descriptive name manually such as London HQ, or click Sequence to have Talos generate a unique numeric ID automatically. This is useful for large deployments. Scroll to the Address pane and enter the physical location. Ensure the address matches what is configured in GCXONE for accurate synchronization. Optionally add custom fields such as License Plate Number or Site Manager Cell to use in automated workflows. Virtual Devices & Connections The Virtual Devices tab lists all receivers and connection points in the Talos cloud. Green Icon: Healthy connection. Red Icon: Disconnected hardware. Dual Path (DP): High-security devices using both Ethernet and mobile or cellular backups for redundancy.

title: Tenant-Customer-Site-Device Model
excerpt: Tenant-Customer-Site-Device Model The GCXONE platform uses a 5-level hierarchical structure to organize security assets, manage access control, and ensure data isolation. Understanding this hierarchy is essential for effective platform administration
content: Tenant-Customer-Site-Device Model The GCXONE platform uses a 5-level hierarchical structure to organize security assets, manage access control, and ensure data isolation. Understanding this hierarchy is essential for effective platform administration and configuration. The 5-Level Hierarchy Structure The hierarchy flows from top to bottom as follows: Tenant → Customer → Site → Device → Camera/Sensor. Each level is nested inside the one above it, creating a clean organizational structure that enforces data isolation at every layer. Level 1: Tenant The Tenant is the top-level organization with completely isolated data and configuration. Complete Data Isolation: Each tenant operates in its own dedicated space. Independent Configuration: Custom settings, branding, and integrations per tenant. Subdomain Access: Each tenant gets a unique subdomain such as company.nxgen.cloud. Multi-Customer Support: A single tenant can serve multiple customers, making it ideal for the MSSP model. Common use cases include Managed Security Service Providers (MSSPs), large enterprises with multiple divisions, and service providers offering white-label solutions. Level 2: Customer The Customer represents individual clients or sub-organizations within a tenant. Client Organization: A distinct organization or business unit under the tenant. Isolated Data: Customer data is completely separate from other customers. Custom Configuration: Site-specific settings and device configurations per customer. User Management: Customer-level user accounts and permissions. Common use cases include individual businesses served by an MSSP, different divisions within a large enterprise, and separate legal entities under a parent organization. Level 3: Site A Site is a physical location such as a building, facility, or geographic area. Physical Location: Includes a real-world address and geographic coordinates. Talos Synchronization: Sites automatically sync with Talos CMS via MQTT messaging. Site names, addresses, contact info, coordinates, and configuration parameters are kept consistent in real time with bidirectional synchronization and error handling. Device Grouping: All devices at a location are grouped under their site. Address Mapping: Used for mapping, routing, and location services. Note: To delete a site, you must be at the Customer level. Level 4: Device A Device is the security hardware at a site — such as an NVR, DVR, alarm panel, or IoT gateway. Device types supported: NVR (IP camera recording), DVR (analog camera recording), Alarm Panels (intrusion detection), and IoT Gateways (smart sensors). Each device requires the following configuration: a unique Server Unit ID, IP address and port, authentication credentials, and NTP configuration. Important: Each device must have a unique Server Unit ID. Duplicate IDs cause alarm attribution conflicts. Level 5: Camera / Sensor The Camera or Sensor is the individual channel connected to a device. Sensor types include video cameras, motion sensors, door sensors, and IoT sensors. Individual Channel: Each sensor occupies a separate channel on its parent device. Automatic Discovery: Sensors are discovered automatically when a device is added. Alarm Attribution: Each sensor can independently trigger alarms and events. Capabilities per sensor may include live stream, playback, PTZ control, and analytics such as motion detection and line crossing. Common Hierarchy Patterns Single Organization: One tenant contains one customer, which contains one or more sites. Multi-Location Business: One tenant contains one customer with multiple sites — ideal for retail chains or enterprises with branch offices. MSSP Model: One tenant contains multiple customers, each with their own sites and devices — the standard model for managed security service providers. Integration with Talos CMS GCXONE and Talos are automatically synchronized by default. Sites created in GCXONE appear directly in Talos. Alarms are attributed to the correct site and device, and events are correlated based on hierarchy relationships. If a specific site is not fully synced, you can manually force synchronization from the site actions menu using Push to Talos (sends data from GCXONE to Talos) or Pull from Talos (retrieves data from Talos to GCXONE). Best Practices Use clear, consistent naming conventions across all levels. Ensure site addresses are accurate for Talos synchronization. Always assign unique Server Unit IDs to avoid alarm attribution conflicts. Organize sites and devices logically to reflect real-world structure. Document tenant-specific configurations for future reference. Avoid reusing Server Unit IDs, using vague names, skipping address validation, or ignoring hierarchy when setting permissions.

content:
Inviting Users Onboarding new team members is handled through an automated email workflow. This ensures that every user securely verifies their identity and sets their own private credentials. Step 1: Navigate to Users
Open Settings
Click on Users
Click Invite New User to start adding a new user Step 2: Enter User Information Fill in the required fields: Personal Information:
First Name
Last Name
Email Address (required — serves as login ID and must be unique)
Phone Number (optional — for SMS alerts) Address Information (optional):
Street Name
Building Number
Zip Code
City
Country Step 3: Configure Account Settings Role Assignment: Select the role that defines what this user can access, such as End User, Admin, or Operator. Each role determines which features and actions the user can access. Session Timeout: Set the session timeout period. Default is 30 minutes. Range is 30 to 1440 minutes (0.5 to 24 hours). Step 4: Send Invitation Click Submit. The user will receive two automated emails:
Email Confirmation: Verifies the email is active.
Password Setup Link: Allows the user to create their secure password. What Happens Next? Once you click Submit, the account setup process works as follows:
The user receives an email titled "Changing your password for the NXGEN application."
They click the password reset link in the email.
They create a new password for their account.
Upon first login, they are directed to the appropriate section based on their role. If Dashboard is enabled in their role, they land on the Dashboard. If Dashboard is not enabled, they land on the first accessible section such as Configuration. Multi-Organization Access Users invited to multiple tenants use the same email address. Single Identity: They only need one password. Tenant Selection: Upon login, they will be prompted to choose which organization they want to access for that session. Switching: They can switch between tenants anytime via Settings → Switch Tenant. Troubleshooting Password Setup Link Expired: For security, the password setup link expires after 24 hours. If a user misses this window, you must Resend Invitation from the Users table. Email Not Received: Ask the user to check their Spam or Junk folder for mail from @nxgen.cloud. Login Issues: Ensure the user clicked the Verification Link in the first email before attempting to use the password setup link.

content:
Managing Users Once users are onboarded, administrators can modify their permissions and client visibility as their responsibilities evolve. Viewing and Searching Users
Go to Settings → Users.
Search Bar: Quickly find a user by their name or email. Editing User Access If a team member changes roles or needs to monitor new clients:
Go to Settings → Users.
Locate the user in the User List table.
Click the Actions (···) menu next to the user.
You will see three options:
Edit User — Update personal information, role, customer group, or session timeout.
Edit User Entities — Assign or modify which customers and sites this user can access using the entity tree with Override or Merge mode.
Remove User — Permanently remove the user from the platform.
Select Edit User to update the following:
First Name and Last Name
Email Address
Phone Number
Address Information
Genesis Role and GCXONE Role
Customer Group
Session Timeout
Click Update User to save changes. To update entity access specifically, select Edit User Entities from the Actions menu. This opens the entity tree where you can assign customers and sites to the user using Override or Merge mode. Permission changes take effect immediately. The user may need to refresh their browser tab to see new sidebar icons or client sites. Remove User When a user no longer needs access, locate the user in the User List table, click the Actions (···) menu, and select Remove User. Removing a user permanently removes their record from the platform. The account cannot be restored after deletion. Note: Use Remove User only when you are certain the user no longer needs access. For temporary access removal, consider updating their role to End User or restricting their entity access instead. Security Best Practices Quarterly Audits: Review your user list every 3 months. Remove or restrict access for anyone who hasn't logged in recently. Offboarding: Remove user access the moment a team member leaves the organization. Avoid Shared Accounts: Never share a single login between multiple people. This destroys your audit trail.

title: Roles & Permissions
excerpt: Roles & Permissions GCXONE uses Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) to manage what each user can do and what they can see. How It Works A role is the core unit of access control in GCXONE. Each role combines three elements: Role Information & Users Name
content:
Roles & Permissions GCXONE uses Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) to manage what each user can do and what they can see. How It Works A role is the core unit of access control in GCXONE. Each role combines three elements: Role Information & Users Name, description, default toggle, assigned users. Module Privileges Which modules & capabilities users can access. Privileges determine WHAT a user can do. Entity Assignment Which customers, sites, devices, and sensors users can access. Entity access determines WHERE a user can operate. Entity assignments can be configured at two levels:
Role-Level Entities: Entities assigned within the role itself, shared by all users in the role.
User-Level Entities: Entities assigned to individual users through Edit Entity Access, allowing different users in the same role to access different customers or sites. Key Distinction: Entity Access vs. Privileges Privileges (configured in the role) determine WHAT actions a user can perform. Entity access determines WHICH customers, sites, devices, and sensors they can see. User-level customization is done through entity access only — there are no user-level privilege overrides. Default Roles GCXONE includes pre-configured roles to help you get started quickly. Each role comes with a defined set of module privileges. Entity access can be further customized per user. Super Admin Full platform access with complete control over all modules, users, roles, and settings.
Complete administrative access to all system features
Can manage all users, roles, and permissions
Full access to all entities
Configure system-wide settings and audit logs
One Super Admin per tenant. Cannot be deleted. Admin Administrative access — can manage users, roles, configuration, and reports, but not billing.
Manage users and role assignments
Configure system settings
Create and manage Entity Groups
View audit logs and reports
Cannot access billing
Cannot delete critical system components Operator Operational access — can view and manage configuration and reports but cannot delete or administer users/roles.
Day-to-day monitoring and alarm processing
View dashboards and configuration
Generate and view reports
Handle operational tasks
Cannot delete system components
Limited administrative capabilities Installer Field technician access — can manage devices, sensors, and network configuration.
Manage physical devices and sensors
Configure network settings
Install and set up hardware
Troubleshoot device issues
Cannot manage users or roles End User Read-only access to dashboards, configuration, and reports.
View dashboards and basic configuration
Access basic reports
View assigned cameras and devices
Arm/disarm for assigned devices/sites
No ability to change settings or administer the system
Figure 1 You can use these default roles as-is, customize them, or create new roles from scratch. Core Concepts Entity Hierarchy GCXONE organizes entities in a hierarchical structure: Service Provider (Tenant / Account) → Customer (Organization) → Site (Physical Location) → Device (Hardware) → Sensor (Camera / Detector) Cascading selection: Selecting a customer automatically includes all sites, devices, and sensors beneath it. This ensures new entities are automatically included when using the Include Children toggle and simplifies configuration.
Entity Hierarchy Module Privileges Privileges are organized by platform module. Each module has an Access Page toggle and granular capability settings. Privileges determine WHAT actions a user can perform across the platform.

title: Site Synchronization
excerpt: Site Synchronization GCXONE and Talos are designed to work as a unified system. Site Synchronization ensures that all site data created or updated in GCXONE is automatically reflected in Talos, and vice versa — keeping both platforms consistently ali
content: Site Synchronization GCXONE and Talos are designed to work as a unified system. Site Synchronization ensures that all site data created or updated in GCXONE is automatically reflected in Talos, and vice versa — keeping both platforms consistently aligned without manual intervention. How It Works By default, GCXONE and Talos synchronize automatically. When a new site is created in GCXONE, it appears directly in Talos. The following data is synchronized between the two platforms: Site name and address, contact information, geographic coordinates, configuration parameters, and operational status. Synchronization happens in real time via MQTT messaging, with built-in error handling and retry logic to ensure reliability. Manual Synchronization If a specific site is not fully synced, you can force synchronization manually from the site actions menu: Push to Talos: Sends the latest site data from GCXONE to Talos. Pull from Talos: Retrieves and updates site data from Talos into GCXONE. How to Manually Sync a Site Go to Configuration → Customers and select the relevant customer. Open the Sites tab and locate the site. Click the actions menu (···) next to the site. Select Push to Talos or Pull from Talos depending on the direction needed. Arm & Disarm From the same actions menu, you can also arm or disarm a site directly without navigating to Talos. This is useful for quick operational control during incidents or maintenance windows. When to Use Manual Sync Manual synchronization is needed when a site was recently created but has not yet appeared in Talos, when site details were updated in one platform but not reflected in the other, or when troubleshooting synchronization issues between the two platforms.

title: Talos User Management
excerpt: Talos User Management While GCXONE manages the account identity, Talos handles the behavioral settings of how an operator interacts with alarms. For more detailed information about Talos user management, visit the Evalink Documentation at documentati
content:
Talos User Management While GCXONE manages the account identity, Talos handles the behavioral settings of how an operator interacts with alarms. For more detailed information about Talos user management, visit the Evalink Documentation at documentation.evalink.io Users Overview Users are company members and other individuals who can work in Talos under the Company account. Talos operates the following user-related terms:
User Roles
User Types
User Profiles Only Administrators can invite and manage users, create new User Roles and User Types. User Roles Each user has a role that defines their level of permissions. There are four preset User Roles in Talos. They cannot be changed or deleted. Administrators can also create custom User Roles based on the preset User Roles. A custom User Role allows you to configure a custom set of permissions. You can view all User Roles for your company under Company → Settings → Users. The following preset User Roles exist in Talos: Administrator Has a full set of permissions. There can be several Administrators under one Company. Manager Intended for managing the work of users with Operator and Operator Minimal User Roles. Like Administrator, Manager can create and manage major Talos objects such as workflows, sites, site groups, and schedules, but access to some features is restricted. For example, Manager cannot invite users or edit global alarm settings. Operator Intended for alarm processing. Operator has access to all Talos interface pages but for the most part cannot create or manage objects — only has permissions for viewing. Operator Minimal Also intended for alarm processing. Compared to Operator, Operator Minimal has access to a limited number of interface pages. Both Operator and Operator Minimal can create and edit certain Talos objects if required to do so by a workflow — for example, if the Add Schedule Entry step is included in a manual workflow. User Types User Types allow fine-tuning of user experience by combining User Roles with certain pre-configured work environments such as Test Types and Workspaces, and access permissions such as selected access to Site Groups in your company. User Types narrow down the flow of incoming alarms for each user in order to more effectively distribute alarms between available operators. There are four preset User Types in Talos. They cannot be deleted, but Administrators can change their settings. Administrators can also add and delete custom User Types. The following preset User Types exist in Talos: Administrator Default User Role: Administrator. Default Workspace: Default. Default Test Type: System. Manager Default User Role: Manager. Default Workspace: Default. Default Test Type: System. Operator Default User Role: Operator. Default Workspace: Default. Default Test Type: System. Operator Minimal Default User Role: Operator Minimal. Default Workspace: Default. Default Test Type: System. Note: User Role is a kernel setting that defines available user permissions. User Type is the main user-defining quality — it combines a User Role and available working environments (Workspaces and Test Types). When Administrators invite new users, they select a User Type for them when issuing an invite. User Profiles A User Profile is the Talos user interface page where you can view all your user information and manage your user activity. To access your user profile, click the user profile icon in the upper right corner and select Profile from the dropdown menu. The following tabs are available on the User Profile page: Profile: View all your user details, monitor and manage your user activity and sessions, and perform basic user actions. Company Management: View which companies you are a member of, accept invites, and switch between companies without logging out and back in. How Users Are Created To create a new user, an Administrator sends an invite to the user. The only prerequisite required is a valid email address. The user then logs in to Talos following the instructions in the invitation and verification emails. After the first user login, the record of the user appears in the list on Company → Users. When inviting a user, the Administrator selects a User Type from the list of available types which includes preset and custom User Types. After a user accepts the invite and logs in, Administrators can change their User Type. Managing Your User Profile Change User Name
In the Profile tab of the User Profile page, scroll to Profile Actions and select Change Name.
Type your First Name and Last Name in the corresponding fields. Both fields are required. You must enter at least 3 characters for each.
Click Let's Start. Talos will log you out for the changes to take effect. Log back in to continue. Change User Password
In the Profile tab, scroll to Profile Actions and select Change Password. Talos sends an email to your login address.
Follow the link in the email.
Enter your new password in both fields so they match. Your password must be

title: Talos Workflows
excerpt: Talos Workflows Workflows are the brain of Talos. They define exactly how an incoming alarm should be handled — whether it's automatically closing a test signal or guiding an operator through a high-priority burglary response. How Workflow Priority W
content: Talos Workflows Workflows are the brain of Talos. They define exactly how an incoming alarm should be handled — whether it's automatically closing a test signal or guiding an operator through a high-priority burglary response. How Workflow Priority Works An alarm is always processed for a specific site. Talos searches for a matching workflow in the following priority order: Site Level: The most specific — workflows configured directly for a single site take highest priority. Site Group: Workflows applied to a group of sites. Global/Company Level: The default fallback workflow applied when no site-level or group-level workflow matches. Creating a Workflow Step 1 — Define Incoming Conditions: Set the triggers that activate the workflow. This includes the alarm code such as BA for Burglary, the schedule such as After Hours only, and the logic — use All of (AND) for strict matching or One of (OR) for broad categories. Step 2 — Add Decision Nodes: Ask the system or the operator a question. Examples include automated checks such as whether the site is currently armed, or manual checks such as whether video shows a human presence. Step 3 — Configure Actions: Define what happens next. Options include sending an automated SMS or email notification, triggering a siren or locking a door, or escalating the alarm to the priority operator queue. Step 4 — Close and Log: Every workflow must end with a Closing Status such as False Alarm or Police Dispatched. This ensures cleaner reporting and a complete audit trail. The Operator Alarms Page The Alarms page is the central cockpit for monitoring staff. The left side displays all unassigned alarms that have arrived but have not yet been picked up by an operator. The right side displays columns for each online operator, showing their current active workload and status — Online or Offline. Bell Icon: Triggers a manual test alarm to verify workflow logic. Phone Icon: Manually starts a Call Workflow when a customer contacts the monitoring center directly. Automated vs. Manual Workflows Use automated workflows for technical signals such as low battery alerts. If the issue does not resolve after a set time, configure the workflow to escalate to a manual workflow for human intervention. This keeps operators focused on situations that genuinely require their attention.

title: What is Evalink Talos?
excerpt: What is Evalink Talos? Evalink Talos is a sophisticated, cloud-based alarm management platform designed specifically for monitoring stations and enterprise security teams. Overview In the NXGEN ecosystem, Talos serves as the Alarm Verification and Di
content: What is Evalink Talos? Evalink Talos is a sophisticated, cloud-based alarm management platform designed specifically for monitoring stations and enterprise security teams. Overview In the NXGEN ecosystem, Talos serves as the Alarm Verification and Dispatch Engine. While GCXONE handles the video analytics, device management, and AI processing, Talos provides the workflows operators use to process alarms, contact customers, and document incidents. Together, GCXONE and Talos form a complete security solution — GCXONE detects and filters events using AI, and Talos ensures the right operator takes the right action at the right time. Core Capabilities Intelligent Alarm Processing: Talos handles real-time alarm reception and prioritizes alarms based on custom business logic. This ensures that a Panic Button alert always reaches an operator before a Loitering alert. Intuitive Operator Interface: The Talos dashboard is optimized for high-stress environments. Operators get one-click actions for rapid incident closure, embedded GCXONE video streams directly inside the alarm card, and mobile access for managers to review site status and history from any iOS or Android device. Reporting & Compliance: Every action taken by an operator — from opening an alarm to calling a technician — is logged in an immutable audit trail, ensuring full compliance with industry standards. Key Benefits 80% False Alarm Reduction: Integrated AI filters out environmental noise before alarms reach operators. Site Auto-Sync: Sites created in GCXONE appear in Talos automatically. Centralized Admin: Manage users and permissions across both platforms from one place. Zero Infrastructure: No on-premise servers or maintenance required. Scalable Pricing: Pay only for sites that are actively being monitored. Faster Training: The intuitive UI reduces onboarding time for new operators.

content: February 2026 Release B Device configuration and operator guide updates. Bulk device configuration  Installer guide restructure  Platform SSO login fix

title: February 2026 Release A
excerpt: API integrations and knowledge base improvements.
content: February 2026 Release A API integrations and knowledge base improvements. Webhook retry logic  Knowledge base search index

title: Operator shift handover notes
excerpt: Structured handover notes field that operators complete at end-of-shift, attached to the active alarm queue state.
content: Operator shift handover notes Structured handover notes field that operators complete at end-of-shift, attached to the active alarm queue state. Reduces missed alarms during shift changeover — top feedback item from Q4 customer survey.

title: Automated compliance report generation
excerpt: Schedule and auto-generate compliance reports in PDF format on a weekly or monthly cadence.
content: Automated compliance report generation Schedule and auto-generate compliance reports in PDF format on a weekly or monthly cadence. Eliminates 4+ hours/month of manual report preparation for compliance teams.

title: Multi-language support (Phase 1)
excerpt: UI language switching for English and Spanish — targeted at Latin American market.
content: Multi-language support (Phase 1) UI language switching for English and Spanish — targeted at Latin American market. Opens the platform to Spanish-speaking operators; required for two pending enterprise contracts.

title: API rate limit visibility
excerpt: Show current API usage and rate limit status in the developer dashboard.
content: API rate limit visibility Show current API usage and rate limit status in the developer dashboard. Prevents unexpected API throttling by giving integrators real-time usage visibility.

title: Advanced reporting filters
excerpt: Date range, custom field filters, and filter preset saving for reports.
content: Advanced reporting filters Date range, custom field filters, and filter preset saving for reports. Reduces manual data export effort by 60% for weekly reporting workflows.

title: New alarm grouping view
excerpt: Group active alarms by zone or device type for faster operator triage.
content: New alarm grouping view Group active alarms by zone or device type for faster operator triage. Reduces mean time to acknowledge alarms by 40% in high-volume environments.

title: Bulk device configuration
excerpt: Allow operators to configure multiple devices simultaneously from a single panel — reduces setup time for large deployments.
content: Bulk device configuration Allow operators to configure multiple devices simultaneously from a single panel — reduces setup time for large deployments. Reduces device setup time by 80% for deployments with 50+ devices.
